elements.sent-2411-001: When watching a video, blind and low vision users can press the C or D keys to activate concise and detailed descriptions generated by a multimodal large language model.
elements.sent-2411-002: In the user study, participants activated descriptions based on different use cases such as visual references or audio cues in the main audio track.

The user presses the “D” key to activate a 'Detailed description,' which describes the woman’s displeased expression and outfit (a strapless black top and sequined skirt), with red crosses emphasizing her message.

elements.sent-2411-005: At the bottom, another video (Pets Animals) shows a dog ascending an A-frame in an agility course.

elements.sent-2411-008: Audio descriptions (AD) make videos accessible for blind and low vision (BLV) users by describing visual elements that cannot be understood from the main audio track.
elements.sent-2411-009: AD created by professionals or novice describers is time-consuming and lacks scalability while offering little control to BLV viewers on description length and content and when they receive it.
elements.sent-2411-010: To address this gap, we explore user-driven AI-generated descriptions, where the BLV viewer controls when they receive descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-011: In a study, 20 BLV participants activated audio descriptions for seven different video genres with two levels of detail: concise and detailed.
elements.sent-2411-012: Our results show differences in AD frequency and level of detail BLV users wanted for different videos, their sense of control with this style of AD delivery, its limitations, and variations among BLV users in their AD needs and perception of AI-generated descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-013: We discuss the implications of our findings for future AI-based AD tools.
elements.sent-2411-014: The rapid growth and popularity of short videos on online video-sharing platforms have exacerbated the accessibility gap for blind and low vision (BLV) users.
elements.sent-2411-015: With billions of users using platforms such as YouTube, TikTok, and Instagram, videos make up 82\% of all internet traffic~\cite{synthesia_video_statistics, hubspot_video_consumption .}
elements.sent-2411-016: As a result, video content has become more diverse, with various types of user-generated content and varying video and audio quality.
elements.sent-2411-017: To make videos accessible, audio descriptions (AD) are recorded by professional describers to narrate the key visual elements of a video, such as actions, characters, scene changes, on-screen text, and other visual content~\cite{visual_made_verbal .}
elements.sent-2411-018: While community platforms, such as YouDescribe~\cite{youdescribe_website , allow sighted describers to volunteer and describe video content, authoring pre-recorded ADs for the ever-growing number of online videos is simply not an option anymore.}
elements.sent-2411-019: Recent advances in artificial intelligence (AI) models, particularly multimodal large language models (MLLMs), offer new automated methods for generating descriptions~\cite{shortscribe,spica, wang_auto_ad, automated_AD,cineAD,chuang2023clearvid,nguyen2024oscar,liu2024artificial .}
elements.sent-2411-020: However, further research is needed to understand BLV users' preferences and requirements for AI-generated ADs across different types of videos.
elements.sent-2411-021: MLLMs can analyze one or more frames from a video and generate a textual description of the visual content~\cite{LLAVA, openai_gptv_system_card, anthropic_claude, deepmind_gemini .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Despite this, the effectiveness of AI-generated descriptions in directly serving BLV users remains largely unknown.
elements.sent-2411-023: The timing of ADs poses another challenge for automated systems.
elements.sent-2411-024: While these systems can detect scene changes, they struggle with short videos, particularly those that are single-shot or feature rapid scene transitions~\cite{video_scene_detection .}
elements.sent-2411-025: While some guidelines for AD timing exist (e.g., ``Avoid describing over audio that is essential to comprehension.''~\cite{dcmp_description_key ), professional describers often rely on their tacit knowledge and the video content to time ADs in a way that supports BLV users' comprehension and enjoyment.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Little data is available on how frequently ADs are needed for different types of videos.
elements.sent-2411-027: In addition, recent qualitative studies suggest that BLV users have diverse AD needs depending on video content (e.g., entertainment vs.\ educational videos) and personal preferences~\cite{jiang2024s, wang_auto_ad .}
elements.sent-2411-028: Still, human and automated systems generally produce fixed descriptions at set times, which might not account for a BLV viewer's preference.
elements.sent-2411-029: Therefore, more research is needed to inform the design of AI-based AD platforms.
elements.sent-2411-030: To address these gaps, we investigate user-driven AI-generated descriptions to gather BLV users' AD needs for different types of videos and analyze their perceptions of watching videos using this approach.
elements.sent-2411-031: User-driven ADs allow BLV viewers to activate a description at any point in the video based on their preferences and audio cues.
elements.sent-2411-032: AI-generated descriptions can be verbose and inefficient for video watching~\cite{verbositybiaspreferencelabeling .}
elements.sent-2411-033: On the other hand, detailed descriptions may improve BLV users' understanding of visual content.
elements.sent-2411-034: Thus, we examine two levels of detail for ADs: a shorter, concise description vs.\ a more detailed description.
elements.sent-2411-035: Specifically, we aim to address the following questions: (1) How do BLV users' preferences for AD timing and detail differ between different video genres? (2) What are BLV individuals' perceptions and experiences with user-driven AI-generated ADs? To answer these questions, we developed a prototype for activating ADs and conducted a study with 20 BLV users across different video genres.
elements.sent-2411-036: We prompted an MLLM, specifically GPT-4 Vision (GPT-4V)~\cite{openai_gptv_system_card , with AD guidelines from professional describers to generate descriptions for seven short online videos spanning various categories, such as film and animation, education, and cooking.}
elements.sent-2411-037: BLV users could press a key to activate either a concise or detailed description at any time.
elements.sent-2411-038: They interacted with the videos to activate ADs, rated the effectiveness, efficiency, and enjoyment of the ADs, and shared their experience with user-activated AI descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-039: Our quantitative results highlight significant differences in the frequency of descriptions needed for different video genres, with shorter AD intervals required for Film and Animation, and longer intervals for Education, Health and Fitness, and Beauty videos.
elements.sent-2411-040: Moreover, BLV individuals differed in their frequency and type of AD requests.
elements.sent-2411-041: Our thematic analysis of the interviews identified three key themes: (1) BLV users' increased sense of control and active watching experience when activating ADs, accompanied by a higher cognitive workload, (2) preferences for pre-recorded vs.\ user-driven ADs depending on the video content, viewing context, and individual BLV users, and (3) the positive and negative aspects of AI-generated descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-042: Drawing on these results, we discuss implications for future AI-based AD platforms and the evolving roles of BLV users and sighted describers.. Our contributions include:.
elements.sent-2411-043: Insights into BLV individuals' perceptions of user-driven AI descriptions when watching short online videos.
elements.sent-2411-044: Empirical data on the frequency and type of AD requests for seven video genres as well as variations among BLV users.
elements.sent-2411-045: We review prior work on video accessibility practices and needs and interactive tools for the creation and use of ADs.
elements.sent-2411-046: Although AD has been around for over three decades and produced for high-budget films and movies~\cite{visual_made_verbal,adaspedagolicaltool , the field has gained momentum in the past decade due to the exponential amount of online videos~\cite{mazur2021audio .}}
elements.sent-2411-047: The WCAG 2.0 Level AA compliance mandates that AD be provided for all prerecorded video content in synchronized media~\cite{wcag_audio_desc, acb_audio_description .}
elements.sent-2411-048: This push for video accessibility has resulted in AD authoring guidelines~\cite{3play_audio_description, dcmp_description_key, netflix_style_guide, ofcom_access_services, acb_guidelines , such as Netflix, which focuses on entertainment content, and DCMP, focusing on educational/instructional content.}
elements.sent-2411-049: These guidelines, initially designed for professional describers, have been used to train novice describers on community-driven platforms~\cite{youdescribe_website .}
elements.sent-2411-050: Yet, these guidelines and practices primarily focus on pre-recorded ADs.
elements.sent-2411-051: Furthermore, UK Ofcom guidelines state that ``some programmes are too fast-moving, or offer little opportunity to insert AD''~\cite{comparativestudyAD , indicating a gap in how to describe other forms of video content.}
elements.sent-2411-052: Recent research suggested that BLV people wish to interact with video content in new ways besides pre-recorded ADs.
elements.sent-2411-053: Specifically, Bodi et al.~\cite{automated_AD investigated the viability of providing video accessibility via interactive visual question answering and showed that BLV users requested descriptions more frequently than asking questions.}
elements.sent-2411-054: Chang et al.\ investigated AD for 360$^\circ$ videos and found BLV participants preferred immersive ADs over standard ADs for this format~\cite{omniscribe .}
elements.sent-2411-055: Similarly, SPICA system~\cite{spica enabled BLV users to interactively explore video content.}
elements.sent-2411-056: Participants found the object exploration feature and object-specific sound effects enhanced overall video consumption.
elements.sent-2411-057: These works underscore the importance of moving beyond traditional ADs to offer BLV users a more customised experience.
elements.sent-2411-058: Similarly, our work investigates user-driven descriptions as a way for BLV users to access video content.
elements.sent-2411-059: Others studied variations in BLV users' AD needs depending on their visual impairment and the video content.
elements.sent-2411-060: Chmiel and Mazur~\cite{mazur_ad_preferences examined AD preferences between congenitally blind, non-congenitally blind and low vision participants and found some specific preferences related to character naming and the use of metaphors in AD.}
elements.sent-2411-061: Another study highlighted how the amount of information in AD can impact the experience of BLV users~\cite{fresno , where segmented step-wise descriptions were less cognitively demanding and led to better recall for BLV users.}
elements.sent-2411-062: A recent interview study by Jiang et al.\ suggested that BLV users have different goals and preferences when watching different video genres~\cite{jiang2024s .}
elements.sent-2411-063: For example, BLV users wanted detailed descriptions of people and their appearance in short-form videos of family and friends but not in educational videos.
elements.sent-2411-064: Relatedly, Natalie et al. found that customized ADs can improve BLV users' video understanding, immersion, and information navigation efficiency~\cite{natalie2024audio .}
elements.sent-2411-065: We build on these studies to collect quantitative data on the frequency and amount of detail needed for seven video genres across users with various visual impairments.
elements.sent-2411-066: To assist sighted users in creating descriptions, several tools have been built to streamline different parts of the process~\cite{live_describe,rescribe,crossa11y,kobayashi,yuksel2020HIML,viscene,accessiblead,natalie_feedback, pausesAD,yoon2019video .}
elements.sent-2411-067: LiveDescribe was one of the first tools that investigated the potential of using volunteers to create audio descriptions by automating gaps to fit the audio description~\cite{live_describe .}
elements.sent-2411-068: Rescribe helped describers optimize description placement by dynamic programming~\cite{rescribe , whereas CrossA11y assisted authors in detecting visual and auditory accessibility issues in a video~\cite{crossa11y .}}
elements.sent-2411-069: Yuksel et al. developed a system that generated baseline descriptions which could then be revised by sighted individuals to produce high-quality descriptions~\cite{yuksel2020HIML .}
elements.sent-2411-070: Other tools have included BLV users in AD and video creation process.
elements.sent-2411-071: Viscene studied the efficacy of collaboration between novice describers and BLV reviewers to create high-quality descriptions~\cite{viscene .}
elements.sent-2411-072: This work informed an automatic feedback tool to support novice users in authoring ADs~\cite{natalie_feedback .}
elements.sent-2411-073: Similarly, AccessibleAD expanded access to AD writing to make BLV users become an active part of AD creation~\cite{accessiblead .}
elements.sent-2411-074: These approaches still require manual authorship, which even with technology support, remains a challenging task for describers.
elements.sent-2411-075: Recent advances in artificial intelligence (AI) models have fueled research in automated AD authorship.
elements.sent-2411-076: Wang et al. built a system that analyzed the audio and visual content of the video to create automated descriptions using deep learning~\cite{wang_auto_ad .}
elements.sent-2411-077: Ihorn et al. developed a hybrid approach that generated descriptions and provided answers or additional information based on user queries~\cite{ihorn2021narrationbot .}
elements.sent-2411-078: Automated AD approaches have been dominated by deep learning, which focused on content extraction (encoding) and text generation (decoding)~\cite{vdsurvey .}
elements.sent-2411-079: Due to the lack of context awareness, the automated descriptions can be rather verbose and or lack detail appropriate to the content of the video.
elements.sent-2411-080: To cater to this, recent systems have used large language models (LLMs) to create descriptions~\cite{shortscribe,spica .}
elements.sent-2411-081: ShortScribe used GPT-4 to create long descriptions, short descriptions, and shot-by-shot descriptions for short-form videos.
elements.sent-2411-082: This is the first of its works that has provided different types of AI descriptions for videos.
elements.sent-2411-083: Similarly, SPICA used GPT-4 to create natural language object descriptions in an interactive video content exploration system~\cite{spica .}
elements.sent-2411-084: These systems used vision models to extract information from individual frames and then created descriptions using LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-085: This two-step approach can disconnect the semantic meaning between the frames and the generated descriptions~\cite{nlxgpt .}
elements.sent-2411-086: Our work builds on this prior research by using an MLLM to directly generate descriptions from a sequence of frames and preserve the spatiotemporal context of the videos.
elements.sent-2411-087: To study user-driven AI descriptions, we selected short videos from different genres, created concise and detailed descriptions for them using GPT-4V, and developed a user interface for watching videos with user-driven ADs.
elements.sent-2411-088: We chose short videos (50-110 seconds) from seven different genres to evaluate user-driven AI descriptions across a wide range of video content.
elements.sent-2411-089: The chosen genres include: Food and Cooking, Beauty, Pets and Animals, People and Blogs, Health and Fitness, Film and Animation, and Education (Figure~\ref{fig:keyframes ).}
elements.sent-2411-090: We selected these genres for several reasons.
elements.sent-2411-091: First, prior literature suggested that BLV users' needs for ADs can vary depending on the video genre~\cite{jiang2024s .}
elements.sent-2411-092: Second, the genres covered a range of visual elements, helping test the MLLMs' descriptive capabilities for different content.
elements.sent-2411-093: Third, these genres varied in audio and speech content, and hence, how frequently ADs are required may vary.
elements.sent-2411-094: Lastly, videos from these genres were frequently accessed and requested on YouDescribe, making them relevant contenders for audio descriptions~\cite{you_described_we_archived .}
elements.sent-2411-095: In these genres, we chose videos that contained speech and visual references and thus could benefit from ADs.
elements.sent-2411-096: We did not include videos that primarily relied on speech or had a music soundtrack.
elements.sent-2411-097: Our initial pilot study with a blind user on 14 different videos suggested that the former category was already accessible and did not require ADs, and the latter category was difficult to use with user-driven ADs due to the limited auditory cues.
elements.sent-2411-098: Thus, we selected videos with a speech track where visual elements were important (e.g., a workout video) or were referenced without explanation~\cite{video_inaccessible .}
elements.sent-2411-099: For example, in the Beauty video, the speaker said, ``stop wearing your top like this,'' while pointing to her top without describing its appearance (Figure~\ref{fig:teaser ).}

elements.sent-2411-100: Our goal was to evaluate the effectiveness of user-driven AI descriptions as a tool for BLV users to watch and interact with videos.
elements.sent-2411-101: However, generating descriptions and speech from visual frames can introduce delays and negatively impact the user experience.
elements.sent-2411-102: Moreover, the output of MLLMs is not deterministic and can change on each API call.
elements.sent-2411-103: Thus, we pre-generated ADs for the videos to run a controlled experiment and minimize the influence of these factors on the results.
elements.sent-2411-104: To generate the descriptions, we collected AD guidelines from online sources and prompted GPT-4V with the guidelines and video frames.. Collecting AD guidelines.
elements.sent-2411-105: We collected AD guidelines from four online sources.
elements.sent-2411-106: These guidelines focused on how to describe educational content ~\cite{dcmp_description_key , entertainment content~\cite{mediaccess,netflix_style_guide , and general guidelines for audio describers~\cite{ofcom_access_services .}}}
elements.sent-2411-107: An example guideline was ``Description should convey facial expressions, body language and reactions, especially when in opposition to the dialogue.
elements.sent-2411-108: These elements can be omitted if they completely mimic the dialogue they are accompanying.''~\cite{netflix_style_guide .}
elements.sent-2411-109: We removed guidelines that focused on timing, context, and audio content of the video, as these aspects cannot currently be used to prompt GPT-4V.
elements.sent-2411-110: This process resulted in 42 guidelines for prompting MLLMs.. Generating descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-111: For each video, we generated two types of ADs, a shorter version (concise) and a longer version (detailed), to let BLV participants choose the level of detail based on their preferences and video content.
elements.sent-2411-112: We created two versions by specifying the maximum length of descriptions in the input prompt: 100 words for detailed descriptions and 25 words for concise descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-113: Based on our iterative prompt-engineering and testing, these limits were the most effective in creating the two types of descriptions without altering the prompt.
elements.sent-2411-114: On average, the detailed ADs were 13 words longer than the concise ADs.
elements.sent-2411-115: For a user-driven experience, we generated an AD for every second of the video using GPT-4V API.
elements.sent-2411-116: Each API call consisted of the prompt and ten frames from the video, with one frame extracted from each second.
elements.sent-2411-117: Thus, each API call described ten seconds of the video, requiring six API calls to describe a one-minute video.
elements.sent-2411-118: We repeated the process twice for the seven videos to get detailed and concise descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-119: During the AD generation process, there were a few instances when the generated descriptions referenced previous descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-120: For instance, in the Film and Animation video, the description for a frame started as: ``Same as description 5''.
elements.sent-2411-121: Because these errors were few and required manual correction for removal, we decided to keep them in the descriptions and assess their impact on BLV users' experience.

elements.sent-2411-122: We prototyped a simple interface to test the user-driven AI descriptions (Figure~\ref{fig:interface ).}
elements.sent-2411-123: We used ReactJs for creating the interface and Firebase for storing all the generated ADs and videos.
elements.sent-2411-124: Participants could press ``C'' on their keyboard to get concise descriptions and ``D'' for detailed descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-125: We used an extended audio description approach, where the video paused when the participant pressed a key, the text-to-speech model read out the description, and then the video playback continued.
elements.sent-2411-126: We used OpenAI's alloy text-to-speech model to read out the descriptions with a natural humanlike tone.
elements.sent-2411-127: The interface logged the key presses (C or D) and their timings.
elements.sent-2411-128: After watching a video, participants could rate their experience and type questions on the interface.
elements.sent-2411-129: We conducted a user study to obtain data on the frequency and type of AD requests across different videos and BLV individuals and elicit user perception of this interactive approach to watching videos.
elements.sent-2411-130: The study was approved by our institution's IRB.
elements.sent-2411-131: The study was conducted over Zoom to facilitate participation from different locations.
elements.sent-2411-132: Each study session took about 75 minutes, and participants received \$50 for their time.

elements.sent-2411-133: We recruited 20 participants (7 female, 13 male) through BLV organizations, Facebook groups, and snowball sampling.
elements.sent-2411-134: The participants were between 27 to 79 years old and had various vision impairments ranging from legal blindness, blindness with some light/color perception, and total blindness (Table~\ref{tab:participants ).}
elements.sent-2411-135: Most participants (n=18) used screen readers to navigate the interface, while two participants used magnification.. { Procedure.}
elements.sent-2411-136: The study session included four phases: completing a demographics survey, a practice segment, video viewing segment with user ratings, and a post-interview segment.
elements.sent-2411-137: After completing a demographic survey, participants interacted with a testing page to get familiar with the interface and used C or D on the keypad to activate descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-138: Then, participants watched the seven videos in random order with user-driven descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-139: They watched each video once and pressed the C and D keys whenever needed to receive an AD for the previous second in the video.
elements.sent-2411-140: The UI logged the time and type of key presses.
elements.sent-2411-141: After each video, participants rated the efficiency, enjoyability, and effectiveness of user-driven ADs on a 5-point Likert-type scale from ``strongly disagree'' to ``strongly agree''.
elements.sent-2411-142: The participants could also type any questions regarding the video.
elements.sent-2411-143: Finally, we conducted a semi-structured interview with participants to gather in-depth feedback on their experience with user-driven ADs and their thoughts on the AI descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-144: The interviews were audio-recorded.. { Data Analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-145: We analyzed the interview transcripts using thematic analysis inspired by Braun and Clarke's approach~\cite{clarke2021thematic .}
elements.sent-2411-146: Specifically, two authors independently applied open coding to each transcript using MAXQDA qualitative analysis software.
elements.sent-2411-147: After coding every five interviews, the two authors met and discussed the similarities and differences between their codes before moving on to the rest.
elements.sent-2411-148: The authors also wrote memos to capture interesting patterns and relationships between codes.
elements.sent-2411-149: After open-coding all the transcripts, the two authors separately identified recurring patterns or themes from the codes and discussed the themes together.
elements.sent-2411-150: One author wrote a draft of the themes, and both authors discussed and revised it by referencing the codes.
elements.sent-2411-151: This process resulted in three themes, each including 2-4 sub-themes (Section~\ref{sec:themes ).}
elements.sent-2411-152: We also applied statistical testing on the user ratings, timing, and type of AD requests (Section~\ref{sec:quant ).}
elements.sent-2411-153: This section presents quantitative results followed by qualitative themes from the interviews.
elements.sent-2411-154: Figure~\ref{fig:ratings shows the distribution of user ratings for the efficiency in watching, effectiveness in comprehending the video, and enjoyability of videos with the user-driven ADs.}
elements.sent-2411-155: The medians for efficiency and effectiveness are 4 (agree), while the median for enjoyability is 3 (neutral).
elements.sent-2411-156: The lower median for enjoyability compared to efficiency and effectiveness suggests that while the descriptions were useful, the overall enjoyment varied across the participants.
elements.sent-2411-157: Participants noted that the extended (rather than inline) presentation of user-driven ADs reduced their enjoyment of the videos.

elements.sent-2411-158: To assess the effect of video genre on user ratings, we ran a Friedman test on each of the three ratings with one factor of video genre (seven levels).

elements.sent-2411-160: The post hoc analysis on efficiency and effectiveness ratings using Wilcoxon signed-rank tests and Holm-Bonferroni correction showed no statistical differences between ratings of different genres.
elements.sent-2411-161: The effect sizes, calculated using Kendall's W, were small for all three ratings which suggests that the video genre had little practical impact on the user ratings.
elements.sent-2411-162: Spearman's rank correlation analysis showed strong correlations between efficiency and effectiveness ($\rho = 0.83$) ratings and between the efficiency and enjoyment ratings ($\rho =0.67$) and a moderate relationship between effectiveness and enjoyment ratings ($\rho =0.54$).
elements.subsubsection-2411-002: Frequency and Type of AD Requests
elements.sent-2411-163: We conducted a repeated measures ANOVA to determine statistical differences in the time intervals between subsequent AD activations (Figure~\ref{fig:interval ).}

elements.sent-2411-166: The results highlight that the frequency of descriptions required can significantly vary for different genres of video content.

elements.sent-2411-174: The frequency and type of AD requests also varied drastically depending on the BLV individuals.

elements.sent-2411-177: Furthermore, the three low-vision participants in our study activated ADs less than the blind participants, perhaps due to relying on their functional vision.
elements.sent-2411-178: These variations further suggest the differing AD needs and preferences of BLV individuals.

elements.sent-2411-179: We identified three themes based on the interviews and observation of participants' experiences with user-driven AI descriptions (Figure~\ref{fig:themes ).}
elements.subsubsection-2411-003: User-driven Description: Redefining Control for Accessibility
elements.sent-2411-180: The concept of control was mentioned repeatedly in participant descriptions of AD activations.
elements.sent-2411-181: The options to say when and how the descriptions were delivered created a sense of control that was missing from pre-recorded ADs.
elements.sent-2411-182: As P15$_{LB}$ highlighted: ``We're unlocking this absolutely mind-blowing layer that we can now add, which is an interactive audio description, the idea of having not only a description of what's going on, but a way to have the description describe to us what we want it to describe to us, how we want it to describe it to us.'' Another participant also echoed this sentiment, noting the limitations of pre-recorded ADs: ``When you have audio description built in, what is described is already predetermined...
elements.sent-2411-183: Participants enjoyed the flexibility of how and when they received ADs, giving them the ability to ``seek information'' (P4$_{B}$) exactly when they wanted it.
elements.sent-2411-184: For example, P6$_{B}$ activated descriptions based on whether they wanted to hear the person in the video or more detailed information about the visual content.
elements.sent-2411-185: Participants likened this user-driven description style to having a live ``human describer''.
elements.sent-2411-186: The ability to control ADs also helped cater to the needs of BLV individuals with varying visual impairments.
elements.sent-2411-187: For example, P15$_{LB}$ expressed their frustration with videos containing on-screen text, highlighting how he has to magnify or use OCR to read text; thus, he could get on-screen text in a more accessible manner using this interaction.
elements.sent-2411-188: In contrast to pre-recorded ADs, the user-driven descriptions made the participants feel more active and engaged when watching videos.
elements.sent-2411-189: With this sense of control, P3$_{LV}$, P7$_{B}$, and P9$_{B}$ imagined creating and saving ADs that they or others could use to watch, summarize, or preview video content later.
elements.sent-2411-191: The increased user control came with its challenges.
elements.sent-2411-192: One of the primary challenges was the disruption of video viewing experience and flow: ``It's easy to forget what was happening in the video if you use the on-demand descriptions too much'' (P17$_{B}$).
elements.sent-2411-193: This disruption was more pronounced for content with greater visual details or little to no silent gaps.
elements.sent-2411-194: The increased control associated with user-activated AD also led to a higher cognitive load.
elements.sent-2411-195: Participants had to actively engage with the video to know when to receive descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-196: This was mentally taxing, especially for content that was information-dense or had fewer silent gaps to activate descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-197: As P16$_{B}$ noted, ``I think the harder ones were things like the dog running through the course (Pets \& Animals).
elements.sent-2411-198: That's really hard because things are changing really fast...
elements.sent-2411-199: User-driven ADs also instilled a fear of missing out (FOMO) visual information in some participants who expressed concern over missing critical information if they activated descriptions infrequently.
elements.sent-2411-200: This concern was particularly felt in fast-paced videos, where they could discern scene changes from audio but could not activate to keep pace with the scene changes: ``I was requesting descriptions too often, because I couldn't make those judgment calls.
elements.sent-2411-201: This sense of FOMO could be a direct result of needing more time with user-driven ADs since it is a new way of receiving descriptions, as highlighted by five participants.
elements.sent-2411-203: Participants had different strategies for activating descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-204: While some felt they activated descriptions randomly without any particular pattern ($n=3$), most noted that they relied on the audio track to decide when to activate an AD ($n=8$).
elements.sent-2411-205: They often avoided activating AD during conversations or speech and waited for a pause or silent gap in the video.
elements.sent-2411-206: Others noted activating descriptions when there were visual references (e.g., ``do it like this'' for the Health \& Fitness video -- P18$_{B}$), when a change in the ambient sounds indicated a scene change (P19$_{B}$), or when they wanted to know the source of sounds in the audio track (e.g., ``Chopping up something, okay what?'' -- P13$_{B}$).
elements.sent-2411-207: User-driven ADs were particularly helpful for videos with difficult speech.
elements.sent-2411-208: For the Food and Cooking video, even though the presenter explained all the steps, the speech was difficult to follow for some participants due to the presenter's accent, and the AD activations helped them understand the visual content better.
elements.sent-2411-210: Some noted that they requested descriptions early in the videos ``to get a context'' (P9$_{B}$, P6$_{B}$, P20$_{B}$).
elements.sent-2411-211: Their interest in the content also affected when and how frequently they requested descriptions, with fewer and concise requests for videos that did not interest them.
elements.sent-2411-212: Finally, low-vision participants also leveraged their functional vision to request ADs, especially to read on-screen text.
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{themes.pdf} \caption{Overview of the three themes and sub-themes from interviews about BLV users' experience with user-driven AI descriptions.} \Description{Infographic summarizing the three key themes from interviews about blind and low vision (BLV) users' experiences with user-driven AI descriptions. The first theme, 'User-driven Descriptions: Redefining Control for Accessibility,' highlights participants' enjoyment of control and flexibility in activating descriptions and challenges like cognitive load and fear of missing important visual details. The second theme, 'Preference for ADs: Dependent on the Video Content, Context, and BLV Individuals,' discusses the preference for pre-recorded ADs in immersive content and the differences in AD needs when watching alone versus socially. The third theme, 'AI: the Good and the Bad,' focuses on AI-generated descriptions, noting that while they provide good visual detail, they sometimes lack context and suffer from hallucinations. AI text-to-speech voices are described as natural and customizable.} ~\label{fig:themes}
elements.sent-2411-213: Participants noted that various forms of ADs (pre-recorded vs. user-driven, concise vs. detailed) supported different uses and that video content and viewing context played a crucial role in their preference for AD type.. { Content dependency.}
elements.sent-2411-214: Conventional or pre-recorded AD format was preferred for entertainment content, where an immersive experience was essential ($n=6$).
elements.sent-2411-215: Conversely, user-driven ADs were useful for content requiring active engagement over immersion, such as educational, workout, and cooking videos, where the ability to request ADs precisely when the action was happening enhanced their understanding of the content ($n=9$).
elements.sent-2411-216: The preference for concise vs. detailed ADs also depended on the video.
elements.sent-2411-217: For fast-paced videos or action sequences, concise descriptions were preferred to get sufficient information while minimally disrupting the video flow.
elements.sent-2411-218: Conversely, participants preferred detailed descriptions for heavily visual content (e.g., Film and Animation), as P9$_{B}$ highlighted: ``The detailed ones to me like they create a visual image in my mind more than you know short ones.'' For instructional videos, participants preferred to have detailed descriptions but noted that the detailed information was unrelated to the context of the video, which reduced the utility of longer descriptions for instructional content.. { Context dependency.}
elements.sent-2411-219: Participants' preference for the form of AD also depended on the viewing context, particularly whether they were watching or re-watching a video and whether they were viewing it alone or in a social setting (i.e., with sighted people).
elements.sent-2411-220: Participants found user-driven descriptions especially useful when re-watching videos.
elements.sent-2411-221: Sometimes, the purpose of re-watching was to get a new perspective or experience on the content.
elements.sent-2411-222: So that part's a huge benefit.'' (P12$_{LB}$).
elements.sent-2411-223: In other cases, they wanted to re-watch the content for specific information.
elements.sent-2411-224: For instance, P19$_{B}$ noted watching do-it-yourself videos with concise ADs first and activating detailed descriptions when re-watching a part of the video later to find specific information.
elements.sent-2411-225: Participants also described different AD preferences when watching alone vs. with sighted people.
elements.sent-2411-226: P6$_{B}$ highlighted that in social contexts, ``the person who is watching with me, a sighted person will be so bored.'' Hence, user-driven would be preferred more in a private setting rather than a social one.
elements.sent-2411-227: Similarly, P12$_{LB}$ noted that they would watch a video with frequent AD activation alone, then ``...watch it with other people and only click, describe occasionally when you're like, oh, I forgot what happened.'' { Individual differences in AD use and preference.}
elements.sent-2411-228: The individual experiences of participants varied widely, which further underscores the importance of adaptable and customized ADs.
elements.sent-2411-229: Participants who appreciated having user-driven AD often cited it as a pleasant, enjoyable experience, and a promising alternative to conventional AD (P1$_{B}$, P6$_{B}$, P14$_{B}$, P17$_{LB}$, P18$_{B}$).
elements.sent-2411-230: P6$_{B}$ noted: ``I was trying to, you know, get as much as possible.''.
elements.sent-2411-231: However, not everyone shared the same narrative, and some found activating descriptions constantly to be overwhelming and unpleasant (P8$_{B}$, P10$_{LV}$, P20$_{B}$).
elements.sent-2411-232: The varied and diverse opinions also translated into how participants activated descriptions, as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:examplerequests .}
elements.sent-2411-233: When asked if they would use user-activated ADs in daily video viewing, the preferred frequency of use varied widely among participants depending on their watching habits.
elements.sent-2411-234: Those who watched instructional and educational videos stated a higher frequency of use: ``it would be nice to kind of get the option...''(P12$_{LB}$).
elements.sent-2411-235: Others, however, felt the disruption that comes with user-driven AD would not be suitable for the content they watch, particularly entertainment content.
elements.sent-2411-236: Hence why, participants felt that user-driven ADs would be more helpful for YouTube videos rather than movies or TV shows (P6$_{B}$, P15$_{LB}$, P19$_{B}$).
elements.sent-2411-237: Also, participants who mentioned lower frequency of use felt that the content they watched was audio-based and did not require AD (P7$_{B}$, P8$_{B}$, P16$_{B}$).
elements.sent-2411-238: These participants also highlighted how they would use it for occasional descriptions: ``Seth Meyers in his show...
elements.sent-2411-239: So when I hear him say something, a pause, and the audience like cracks up, I can run it and say, okay, there's a funny picture being shown of something, so that would be useful.. (P8$_{B$) ''.}
elements.sent-2411-240: Participants had varied opinions about the effectiveness of AI descriptions in the study and in general based on their prior experience and the types of content they watched.
elements.sent-2411-241: Several saw it as a promising future, while some felt skeptical about how well AI can describe different forms of content (P11$_{LB}$, P15$_{LB}$) .
elements.sent-2411-242: Ten participants highlighted the quality of the AI-generated descriptions, particularly appreciating the visual detail.
elements.sent-2411-243: Unlike human ADs, which incur substantial costs in production, AI provided an ``opportunity to describe more content'' .
elements.sent-2411-244: The need for more content to be described was evident, as participants reported watching 11 unique genres.
elements.sent-2411-245: At the same time, they mostly only received descriptions for Film and TV shows.
elements.sent-2411-246: P19$_{B}$ found that AI descriptions will increase the range of content they can watch: ``You know, like the [Legend of] Zelda, I would love to be able to sit there through a full description narration of game play-through.''.
elements.sent-2411-247: Additionally, participants acknowledged the benefit of AI descriptions to allow customization: ``the power of the technology will allow us to give people these choices to give them the concise or more flowery detail...
elements.sent-2411-248: Some participants noted the benefits of AI-generated descriptions over human descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-249: P15$_{LB}$, who enjoyed the user-driven ADs, highlighted how paid sighted workers are often untrained to provide AD and found these AI descriptions were ``fine-tuned'' and had an ``audio description vibe to them''.
elements.sent-2411-250: However, others felt the AI descriptions were similar to novice describers but failed to compete with professional descriptions (P7$_{B}$, P8$_{B}$).
elements.sent-2411-251: Participants also noted that AI may help remove the subjective censorship by human describers.
elements.sent-2411-252: P1$_{B}$ mentioned how narrators are often too careful in describing people and characters to the point where they ``lose some of the experience''.
elements.sent-2411-253: A similar concern was echoed by P11$_{LV}$, who emphasized that descriptions should be on point and ``if you're embarrassed to narrate...
elements.sent-2411-254: I would hope you give your job to someone else.'' These comments illustrate a potential for how AI descriptions can help bridge the gap between unbiased and accurate descriptions, potentially complementary to human AD.
elements.sent-2411-255: AI descriptions could lack the context that human describers would provide, which is important for instructional content.
elements.sent-2411-256: Though the AI descriptions provided visual detail, the descriptions lacked detailed guidance to follow along for instructional and educational content ($n=6$).
elements.sent-2411-257: Participants also noted that some descriptions did not adequately relate to the main audio track and were confusing as they provided more ``static'' rather than dynamic information, exemplified in the Health and Fitness video, as highlighted by P16$_{B}$: ``The motion was important to the content of the video...
elements.sent-2411-258: Even with a structured prompt, some descriptions referenced previous descriptions, which led to confusion as P7$_{B}$ stated that they ``don't know what description 5 was''.
elements.sent-2411-259: Although the prompt explicitly mentioned reading on-screen text central to understanding, the concise descriptions often failed to contain the on-screen text and only mentioned ``on-screen text.'' This further increased FOMO as expressed by P19$_{B}$: ``What if one of the videos have a secret formula written on the screen, and only you know that the viewer...
elements.sent-2411-260: But to the blind person, this just says there's text on the screen.'' AI descriptions were also prone to hallucinations in some cases.
elements.sent-2411-261: The hallucinations were of particular concern for educational settings: ``when things are slightly inaccurate, it shouldn't be used for things like exams (P13$_{B$)''.}
elements.sent-2411-262: Some participants explicitly noted concern with hallucinations in AI (P8$_{B}$).
elements.sent-2411-263: This was true even when AI descriptions were accurate.
elements.sent-2411-264: For example, in the comment for Pets \& Animal video, P18$_{B}$ wrote: ``The dog part was mentioned towards the end of the video.
elements.sent-2411-265: However, the AD mentioned about the dog much before.
elements.sent-2411-266: This was helpful in getting the context into the video.
elements.sent-2411-267: Till the video spoke of dog, I was skeptical that the AI is calling something else a dog.'' These comments indicate lower trust in AI-generated vs. human descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-268: Finally, the descriptions did not adapt to the participants' backgrounds.
elements.sent-2411-269: While some participants enjoyed the visual ``flowery detail (P6$_{B$)'' provided by the descriptions, others highlighted how the descriptions implored too much background information.}
elements.sent-2411-270: For instance, the mention of colors in the description made no sense to P16$_{B}$ as a congenitally blind person.
elements.sent-2411-271: These aspects highlight further expectations for flexibility from AI descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-272: An appreciated aspect of TTS voices was how natural and clear they sounded compared to technologies used by the participants in screen readers.
elements.sent-2411-273: In fact, P1$_{B}$ thought a live person was speaking out the descriptions upon their key presses.
elements.sent-2411-274: Participants enjoyed the tone of the AI voice, highlighting the voice was clear and easy to understand ($n=4$).
elements.sent-2411-275: They appreciated having a more distinct voice for the descriptions from all videos, so ``you get to be comfortable with that voice'' (P2$_{B}$).
elements.sent-2411-276: Several participants also highlighted the customization advantages of AI TTS, particularly the ability to increase speed (P9$_{B}$, P13$_{B}$) and to change between male or female voices based on content (P11$_{B}$, P15$_{LB}$).
elements.sent-2411-277: The most common feedback from participants was wanting to speed up the TTS ($n=9$), noting that a faster TTS would enhance the efficiency of user-driven ADs.
elements.sent-2411-278: Speech quality was less important and only was a source of relevance for entertainment content.
elements.sent-2411-279: Some participants noted AI voices can sound monotonous, lacking the emotional depth that a human describer would provide (P12$_{LB}$, P19$_{B}$).
elements.sent-2411-280: For other video genres, participants preferred a faster TTS to browse the video quickly.
elements.sent-2411-281: Overall, the quality of the AI descriptions was far more important to the participants than the quality of TTS, ``so long as the quality of the description itself is high.
elements.sent-2411-282: Through detailed participant feedback, we identified the benefits and challenges associated with user-driven descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-283: Although participants appreciated the control offered by a user-driven interaction, they highlighted the cognitive load that it incurs.
elements.sent-2411-284: AI descriptions aided visual understanding but lacked context.
elements.sent-2411-285: Additionally, statistical analysis revealed that concise ADs were requested more often, and the time interval between descriptions varied significantly across genres.
elements.sent-2411-286: We discuss the need for an online AI-based AD platform, the evolving role of describers and BLV users in AI-assisted AD, and the potential of multisensory interactions in AD consumption.
elements.subsection-2411-008: Implications for an AI Description Platform
elements.sent-2411-287: An online platform for AI-generated descriptions must offer various forms of ADs for watching online videos.
elements.sent-2411-288: In our study, BLV users watched various online content and wanted to use pre-recorded, user-driven, concise, and detailed ADs depending on the video content, social context, and their individual needs when watching a video.
elements.sent-2411-289: While some preferred having pre-recorded AD for entertainment content, others enjoyed having control over the ADs.
elements.sent-2411-290: Also, information that seemed trivial to some could be significant to others, such as the use of colors in descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-291: The diversity of BLV users' opinions and their patterns of AD activations underscores the need for customization in AI descriptions and AD systems.
elements.sent-2411-292: Relatedly, recent research have investigated various AD types and question-answering for BLV users~\cite{spica,yuksel2020HIML,automated_AD,ihorn2021narrationbot .}
elements.sent-2411-293: These AD approaches can be integrated into an online video-sharing platform (e.g., YouTube or YouDescribe) to enable BLV users to effectively watch diverse video content.
elements.sent-2411-294: With BLV users' permission, such an online platform can collect data on variations in video viewing preferences of BLV users (e.g., AD frequency, type, questions) over time to improve the AI descriptions and learn to time ADs depending on video content and user needs.
elements.sent-2411-295: This crowdsourced approach can further enable new ways for BLV users to skim or re-watch videos or receive video descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-296: While AI descriptions present an opportunity for increasing video accessibility, some nuances need to be taken into account.
elements.sent-2411-297: Descriptions generated using GPT-4-vision were high in accuracy, but a few descriptions still included object hallucinations (e.g., labeling ``pork ribs'' as ``chicken'' in the Food and Cooking video)~\cite{Hallucinations .}
elements.sent-2411-298: This highlights the importance of human verification for AI descriptions when factual correctness cannot be determined via the audio track, or for information critical content~\cite{gulfenvisioningcognitive .}
elements.sent-2411-299: Additionally, contextual relevance can be a source of concern, which makes standalone AI descriptions insufficient for long-form content such as films and documentaries.
elements.sent-2411-300: Furthermore, even with prompt engineering, descriptions generated using MLLMs are prone to consistency issues.
elements.sent-2411-301: Although some inconsistencies might not notably affect the BLV user experience, issues such as self-reference to previous descriptions can increase the cognitive load for BLV users and reduce enjoyment.
elements.sent-2411-302: Addressing accuracy, context and consistency will improve the reliability of using AI descriptions.
elements.subsection-2411-009: Change in Roles for Describers and BLV Users
elements.sent-2411-303: With recent advances in AI descriptions, there is a potential for the roles of sighted volunteers to change from creating descriptions to adjusting the timings of receiving descriptions and removing hallucinations and inaccuracies.
elements.sent-2411-304: Prior work has explored how preset sentence templates can enhance descriptions generated by novice describers for images~\cite{image_templates .}
elements.sent-2411-305: Similarly, creating descriptions with automated feedback~\cite{natalie_feedback and automating video text generation and scene segmentation~\cite{yuksel2020HIML was easier for novice describers and significantly improved the AD quality for BLV users.}}
elements.sent-2411-306: While previous efforts have mainly focused on using AI to support novice describers, with newer MLLMs, the role of describers could shift.
elements.sent-2411-307: In terms of providing visual details, most participants felt the AI descriptions were adequate.
elements.sent-2411-308: However, several participants needed help knowing when to request descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-309: Instead of volunteers working to create descriptions, they can work on when to insert descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-310: Although libraries and models exist that help detect silent gaps in audio for the insertion of descriptions, these fail for short-form content where there are fewer or no silent gaps.
elements.sent-2411-311: Some BLV users also had low trust in AI-generated descriptions which worsened with even small AD inaccuracies.
elements.sent-2411-312: Sighted users can easily detect such hallucinations and inconsistencies and correct them to improve BLV user comprehension, trust, and overall experience.
elements.sent-2411-313: With user-driven AI descriptions, the role of BLV users can also further shift toward from video consumption to active content creation.
elements.sent-2411-314: Some BLV users in our study were excited about the possibilities of user-driven ADs and wanted to save and share ADs (and variations on every re-watching) for the videos.
elements.sent-2411-315: Also, compared to sighted volunteers, low-vision volunteers have a better idea of when descriptions are needed for a video (e.g., on-screen text, visual reference).
elements.sent-2411-316: While there are already BLV content creators~\cite{digitalContentCreationBLV, avscript , a user-driven AD tool can open up the space to a wider range of BLV users to become AD creators, determining when and how ADs should be inserted for different videos based on their lived experience.}
elements.sent-2411-317: This role change can make AD creation an inclusive space for BLV describers.
elements.sent-2411-318: Prior research has looked into using multi-sensory AD approaches for artwork~\cite{multiADVisualArts, accessibleVisualArts, artVoiceControlled and for AD in movies ~\cite{hapticsInMovies, midairhaptics to improve visualization.}}
elements.sent-2411-319: Subjects have also highlighted how they would like additional output modalities such as audio cues, tactile graphics, and haptics to enhance the AD experience for different viewing scenarios (e.g., how-to, short-form, comedy, drama)~\cite{jiang2024s .}
elements.sent-2411-320: Though this requires technology that might not always be available to BLV users, several output modalities can still be incorporated.
elements.sent-2411-321: Our participants wished to know when to activate AD for user-driven style AD.
elements.sent-2411-322: Audio cues, or vibrations, can be incorporated into the video to let the BLV user know when they can activate a description.
elements.sent-2411-323: This way, the control of receiving descriptions still lies with the user, and they have a better idea of when there are scene changes or where they can benefit from activating descriptions, effectively reducing the cognitive load of not knowing when to request descriptions or having too many descriptions in the case of predetermined AD.
elements.sent-2411-324: There is limited work done on the intersection of AD and other modalities, especially for different video content.
elements.sent-2411-325: Future work can explore whether other modalities complement or obstruct BLV users' attention to AD.
elements.sent-2411-327: First, our study covers a subset of video types that BLV users wish to watch.
elements.sent-2411-328: Within a 75-minute study, we could only test seven video genres.
elements.sent-2411-329: Also, all the videos were relatively short.
elements.sent-2411-330: A lot of variation exists in content within each genre, and the pace of video has also likely impacted the quantitative results.
elements.sent-2411-331: Testing user-driven AD with other genres, videos with different paces within a genre, and longer videos can give further insights into the efficacy of user-driven descriptions for different video types.

elements.sent-2411-349: AD created by professionals or novice describers is time-consuming and offers little customization or control to BLV viewers on description length and content and when they receive it.
elements.sent-2411-350: To address this gap, we explore user-driven AI-generated descriptions, enabling BLV viewers to control both the timing and level of detail of the descriptions they receive.
elements.sent-2411-351: Our findings reveal differences in the preferred frequency and level of detail of ADs for different videos, participants' sense of control with this style of AD delivery, and its limitations.
elements.sent-2411-352: We discuss the implications of these findings for the development of future AD tools for BLV users.
elements.sent-2411-353: The rapid growth and popularity of videos on online platforms have widened the accessibility gap for blind and low vision (BLV) users.
elements.sent-2411-354: As a result, video content has become more diverse, with a wide range of user-generated content and varying levels of quality.
elements.sent-2411-355: To make videos accessible, professional describers record audio descriptions (AD) to narrate the key visual elements, such as actions, characters, scene changes, on-screen text, and other relevant content~\cite{visual_made_verbal .}
elements.sent-2411-356: Recent advances in artificial intelligence (AI) models, particularly multimodal large language models (MLLMs), provide automated methods and interfaces for generating descriptions~\cite{shortscribe,spica, wang_auto_ad, automated_AD,cineAD,chuang2023clearvid,nguyen2024oscar, chang2024worldscribe .}
elements.sent-2411-357: MLLMs can analyze a sequence of frames from a video and generate a textual description of the content~\cite{LLAVA, openai_gptv_system_card, anthropic_claude, deepmind_gemini .}
elements.sent-2411-358: However, the effectiveness of AI-generated descriptions in directly serving BLV users remains largely unknown.
elements.sent-2411-359: Recent qualitative studies suggest that BLV users have diverse needs for the frequency and content of ADs depending on the type of video (e.g., entertainment vs.\ educational videos) and personal preferences~\cite{jiang2024s, wang_auto_ad .}
elements.sent-2411-360: To address these gaps, we investigate user-driven AI-generated descriptions to analyze BLV users' perceptions of watching videos using this approach and identify their AD needs for different types of videos.
elements.sent-2411-361: While AI-generated descriptions can be verbose and inefficient for video consumption~\cite{verbositybiaspreferencelabeling , detailed descriptions may enhance BLV users' understanding of visual content.}
elements.sent-2411-362: To understand the utility of this approach, we ask: (Q1) What are BLV individuals' perceptions and experiences with user-driven AI-generated ADs? (Q2) How do BLV users' preferences for AD timing and detail differ between different video genres? To answer these questions, we developed a prototype for activating ADs and conducted a study with 20 BLV users across different video genres.
elements.sent-2411-363: We prompted an MLLM, specifically GPT-4 Vision (GPT-4V)~\cite{openai_gptv_system_card , with AD guidelines from professional describers to generate descriptions for seven short online videos spanning various categories, such as film and animation, Education, and Cooking.}
elements.sent-2411-364: They interacted with the videos to activate ADs, rated the effectiveness, efficiency, and enjoyment of the ADs, and shared their experience with user-driven AI descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-365: Our thematic analysis of the interviews identified three key themes: (1) BLV users' increased sense of control and active watching experience when activating ADs, accompanied by a higher cognitive workload, (2) preferences for pre-recorded vs.\ user-driven ADs depending on the video content, viewing context, and individual BLV users, and (3) the positive and negative aspects of user-driven AI descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-366: In addition, our quantitative results suggest differences in the frequency of descriptions needed for different video genres, with shorter AD intervals required for Film and Animation, and longer intervals for Education and Health and Fitness videos.
elements.sent-2411-367: Drawing on these results, we discuss implications for future user-driven AD platforms and the evolving roles of BLV users and sighted describers.. Our contributions include:.
elements.sent-2411-368: This push for video accessibility has resulted in AD authoring guidelines~\cite{3play_audio_description, dcmp_description_key, netflix_style_guide, ofcom_access_services , such as Netflix, which focuses on entertainment content, and DCMP, focusing on educational/instructional content.}
elements.sent-2411-369: These guidelines, originally designed for professional describers, have been used to train novice describers on community-driven platforms~\cite{youdescribe_website .}
elements.sent-2411-370: Previous work investigating AD for 360$^\circ$ videos found that BLV participants wanted different parts of the visual scene described in addition to the main action~\cite{fidyka2018audio .}
elements.sent-2411-371: Other studies demonstrated that BLV participants preferred immersive ADs over standard ADs for this format~\cite{omniscribe and had diverse preferences for the spatial location of AD~\cite{jiang360videos .}}
elements.sent-2411-372: Similarly, the SPICA system~\cite{spica enabled BLV users to explore video content interactively.}
elements.sent-2411-373: These works highlight the importance of moving beyond traditional ADs to offer BLV users a customized experience.
elements.sent-2411-374: Similarly, our work investigates user-driven descriptions as a way for BLV users to access video content when needed.
elements.sent-2411-375: Another study highlighted how the amount of information in AD can impact the experience of BLV users~\cite{fresno , where segmented stepwise descriptions were less cognitively demanding and led to better recall for BLV users.}
elements.sent-2411-376: Relatedly, Natalie et al.\ found that customized ADs can improve BLV users' video understanding, immersion, and information navigation efficiency~\cite{natalie2024audio .}
elements.sent-2411-377: To assist sighted users in creating descriptions, several tools have been built to streamline different parts of the process~\cite{live_describe,rescribe,crossa11y,kobayashi,yuksel2020HIML,viscene,accessiblead,natalie_feedback, pausesAD .}
elements.sent-2411-378: Other tools have included BLV users in the AD and video creation process.
elements.sent-2411-379: This work informed an automatic feedback tool to support novice describers in authoring ADs~\cite{natalie_feedback .}
elements.sent-2411-380: Recent advances in artificial intelligence (AI) models have fueled research in automated AD authorship~\cite{accessibilityresearch .}
elements.sent-2411-381: Wang et al.\ built a system that analyzed the audio and visual content of the video to create automated descriptions using deep learning~\cite{wang_auto_ad .}
elements.sent-2411-382: Ihorn et al.\ developed a hybrid approach that generated descriptions and provided answers or additional information based on user queries~\cite{ihorn2021narrationbot .}
elements.sent-2411-383: Automated AD approaches have been dominated by deep learning, which focuses on visual content extraction (encoding) and text generation (decoding)~\cite{vdsurvey .}
elements.sent-2411-384: To overcome these limitations, recent systems have used large language models (LLMs) to provide BLV users with agency when watching a video or scene~\cite{shortscribe,spica,chang2024worldscribe,li2025videoa11y .}
elements.sent-2411-385: For example, ShortScribe system utilized GPT-4 to provide three levels of hierarchical visual summaries for short-form videos.
elements.sent-2411-386: Similarly, SPICA used GPT-4 to create natural language descriptions of objects BLV users selected in key frames of a video~\cite{spica .}
elements.sent-2411-387: VideoA11y combined MLLMs with AD guidelines to generate descriptions that matched trained human annotations in clarity, accuracy, objectivity, descriptiveness, and user satisfaction~\cite{li2025videoa11y .}
elements.sent-2411-388: WorldScribe further supported BLV user's agency by providing live visual descriptions about the user's surroundings using various vision language models~\cite{chang2024worldscribe .}
elements.sent-2411-389: Our work builds on this prior research by using an MLLM to generate descriptions that can be activated at any time, to gather insights into how end-user control over description brevity and timing of descriptions will impact BLV users’ experiences.
elements.sent-2411-390: We chose short videos (50--110 seconds) from seven different genres to evaluate user-driven AI descriptions across a wide range of video content.
elements.sent-2411-391: We prioritized genre diversity over the number of videos per genre to account for differences in participant interests.
elements.sent-2411-392: Shorter videos were chosen to minimize the chance of disengagement due to a lack of interest in specific genres.
elements.sent-2411-393: Our genre selection was guided by prior literature, which suggests that BLV users' needs for ADs can vary depending on the video genre~\cite{jiang2024s .}
elements.sent-2411-394: The genres also covered a range of visual elements, helping test the MLLMs' descriptive capabilities for different content.
elements.sent-2411-395: The videos in these genres varied in audio and speech content, and hence, how frequently ADs are required may vary.
elements.sent-2411-396: Additionally, videos from these genres were frequently accessed and requested on YouDescribe, making them relevant contenders for audio descriptions~\cite{you_described_we_archived .}
elements.sent-2411-397: We did not include videos that relied primarily on speech or had a music soundtrack.

elements.sent-2411-403: In our experimentation, directly prompting GPT-4V to create concise and detailed descriptions led to inconsistent results on different API calls.
elements.sent-2411-404: Thus, we created two versions by specifying the maximum length of descriptions in the input prompt: 100 words for detailed descriptions and 25 words for concise descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-405: To determine the length, we evaluated maximum description lengths of 25, 50, 75, 100, and 200 words.
elements.sent-2411-406: The upper limit of 200 sometimes led to excessively verbose and repetitive descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-407: Also, when the lengths of concise and detailed were close (e.g., 50 and 75), the descriptions were often hard to differentiate.
elements.sent-2411-408: Based on iterative prompting and testing, we found 25 and 100 words the most effective in creating the two types of descriptions without altering the prompt.
elements.sent-2411-409: For a user-driven experience, we generated a description for every second of the video using GPT-4V API.
elements.sent-2411-410: Thus, each API call returned ten descriptions that describe the ten consecutive seconds of the video, requiring six API calls (i.e., 60 descriptions) to describe a one-minute video.
elements.sent-2411-411: The same AD guidelines were used for both types of descriptions.. Reviewing description quality.
elements.sent-2411-412: We checked all the descriptions to assess the concise and detailed descriptions for the same scenes and check the overall description quality.
elements.sent-2411-413: The detailed ADs contained more visual detail about the scene, object properties, and people's appearances and described the on-screen text which were often omitted from concise ADs (see Appendix~\ref{app:descriptions ).}

elements.sent-2411-427: The ratings assessed (a) {Efficiency : ``The audio descriptions made watching the video more efficient,'' (b) {Effectiveness : ``The information provided in the audio descriptions was effective in helping to understand the visual content of the video,'' and (c) {Enjoyability : ``The experience of listening to the audio descriptions while watching the videos was enjoyable''.}}}
elements.sent-2411-428: For three of the seven videos, participants were asked to provide a brief summary of the content to encourage users to carefully watch and engage with the videos.
elements.sent-2411-429: Participants could also type any questions related to the video.
elements.sent-2411-430: Finally, we conducted a semi-structured interview with participants to gather in-depth feedback on their experience with user-driven AI descriptions.
