elements.subsubsection-2411-005: {User-driven AI: the Good and the Bad}
elements.sent-2411-456: Participants had varied opinions on the effectiveness of AI descriptions in the study and in general based on their prior experience and the types of content they watched.
elements.sent-2411-457: Several saw it as a promising future, while some felt skeptical about how well AI, even if user-driven, can describe different forms of content (P11$_{LB}$, P15$_{LB}$) .
elements.sent-2411-458: The user-driven approach provided an ``opportunity to describe more content''.
elements.sent-2411-459: The need for more content to be described was evident, as participants reported watching 11 unique genres, while traditional pre-recorded AD is often limited to film and TV shows.
elements.sent-2411-460: P19$_{B}$ found that user-driven AI descriptions will increase the range of content they can watch: ``You know, like the [Legend of] Zelda, I would love to be able to sit there through a full description narration of game play-through.'' Additionally, AI descriptions with a user-driven approach can allow BLV users to efficiently browse diverse video content: ``Just a quick description can let me know what's happening, and I can move on (P14$_{B$).'' Relatedly, participants appreciated how user-driven AI allowed them to meet their unique preferences for the amount of detail: ``the power of the technology will allow us to give people these choices to give them the concise or more flowery detail...}
elements.sent-2411-461: Another significant benefit noted by participants was that user-driven AI AD can help eliminate subjective censorship by human describers.
elements.sent-2411-462: I would hope you give your job to someone else.'' Ten participants appreciated the visual detail provided by the descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-463: By giving BLV users access to activate AI descriptions on demand, the user-driven approach can help mitigate concerns of subjectivity inherent in pre-recorded human descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-464: Participants highlighted several challenges with user-driven AI descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-465: Participants expressed that athough they provided visual detail, the descriptions lacked detailed guidance to follow along for instructional and educational content ($n=6$).
elements.sent-2411-466: This resulted in the AD activation providing more ``static'' rather than dynamic information, as noted by P16$_{B}$ in the Health and Fitness video: ``The motion was important to the content of the video...
elements.sent-2411-467: But to the blind person, this just says there's text on the screen.'' Participants mentioned AI hallucinations in some instances.
elements.sent-2411-468: The hallucinations were of particular concern for educational settings, as P13$_{B}$ explains: ``when things are slightly inaccurate, it shouldn't be used for things like exams...
elements.sent-2411-469: Till the video spoke of dog, I was skeptical that the AI is calling something else a dog.'' These comments indicate a lower trust in AI-generated over human descriptions, which could hinder the adoption of a user-driven AI platform for several BLV participants (n=4).
elements.sent-2411-470: Finally, while this approach provided agency over timing and detail, the descriptions did not adapt to the participants' backgrounds.
elements.sent-2411-471: These aspects highlight further expectations for flexibility and customization from AI descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-472: The ability to adjust text-to-speech voices is particularly important for BLV users when it comes to user-driven interaction.
elements.sent-2411-473: Several participants also highlighted the customization advantages of AI TTS, particularly the ability to change between male or female voices based on the content (P11$_{B}$, P15$_{LB}$).
elements.sent-2411-474: However, some participants noted AI voices can sound monotonous, lacking the emotional depth that a human describer would provide (P12$_{LB}$, P19$_{B}$), which was important for entertainment content for an immersive experience.
elements.sent-2411-475: For most other content, participants preferred a faster TTS to quickly browse the video.
elements.sent-2411-476: Overall, the quality and customization of AI descriptions was far more important to the participants than the TTS, ``so long as the quality of the description itself is high, that's all that matters'' (P19$_{B$).}
elements.sent-2411-477: In this paper, we examined two questions: (Q1) What are BLV individuals' perceptions and experiences with user-driven AI-generated ADs? (Q2) How do BLV users' preferences for AD timing and detail differ between different video genres? Regarding Q1, we identified the benefits and challenges associated with user-driven descriptions through detailed participant feedback.
elements.sent-2411-478: Participants appreciated the control and flexibility offered by user-driven interaction, but also highlighted the cognitive load and fear of missing out that it incurs.
elements.sent-2411-479: Participants appreciated the opportunity to request descriptions for more diverse content, such as instructional videos and for re-watching content, and noted that user-driven descriptions might be less suitable for entertainment content, particularly when watching with others.
elements.sent-2411-480: There was also an interest for greater customization, such as adjusting the voice or tailoring descriptions to individual preferences.
elements.sent-2411-481: For Q2, concise ADs were requested more often in our study, and the time interval between descriptions varied significantly across genres, with entertainment content requiring more frequent descriptions when compared to instructional content.
elements.sent-2411-482: These results provide insights into the utility of user-driven AI ADs and provide an avenue for further using the approach for a larger population of BLV users, as well as for diverse video content, to further assess emerging trends.
elements.sent-2411-483: We discuss the need for an online user-driven AI description platform, the evolving role of describers and BLV users in AI-assisted AD authoring, and the potential of multisensory interactions in AD consumption.
elements.subsection-2411-012: Implications for a User-Driven AI Description Platform
elements.sent-2411-484: A user-driven platform for AI-generated descriptions must offer customization of ADs, and various interactivity styles to meet the diverse needs of BLV users.
elements.sent-2411-485: These AD approaches can be integrated into an online video-sharing platform (e.g., YouTube or YouDescribe) to provide agency to BLV users in watching diverse video content.
elements.sent-2411-486: This crowd-sourced approach can further enable new ways for BLV users to skim or re-watch videos or receive video descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-487: While user-driven AI descriptions present an opportunity for improving video accessibility, their real-time implementation introduces several practical challenges.
elements.sent-2411-488: In our study, we pre-generated descriptions to mitigate latency and provide a seamless viewing experience.
elements.sent-2411-489: However, generating descriptions live using LLMs can be time-consuming, especially for longer videos, and may lead to interruptions in the video viewing experience.
elements.sent-2411-490: One way to support user-driven interaction is to generate descriptions for all frames in advance, prior to the interaction.
elements.sent-2411-491: This introduces an initial delay in video preparation instead of during playback.
elements.sent-2411-492: While this delay may only take a few minutes for short videos, longer-form content could require significantly more time.
elements.sent-2411-493: Nonetheless, we anticipate that this latency will decrease as LLMs continue to evolve.
elements.sent-2411-494: Furthermore, to reduce cognitive load and prevent excessively long viewing sessions, these pre-generated descriptions can be dynamically grouped in real time into intervals based on the user's previous activations for the video, ensuring that essential information is not missed without requiring constant interaction.
elements.sent-2411-495: This approach minimizes the need for frequent activations while maintaining accessibility and reducing cognitive burden.
elements.sent-2411-496: Nuances related to the accuracy and consistency of AI descriptions also need to be taken into account for AI-based AD platforms.
elements.sent-2411-497: Descriptions we generated using GPT-4-vision are high in accuracy, but a few descriptions still include object hallucinations (e.g., labeling ``pork ribs'' as ``chicken'' in the Food and Cooking video)~\cite{Hallucinations .}
elements.sent-2411-498: This highlights the importance of developing mechanisms to control the generation process and use human verification for AI descriptions when factual correctness cannot be determined via the audio track, or for critical information~\cite{gulfenvisioningcognitive .}
elements.sent-2411-499: In addition, MLLMs generate different outputs each time, raising questions about ensuring the quality of user-driven ADs.
elements.sent-2411-500: Contextual relevance can be another source of concern, especially for long-form content such as films and documentaries.
elements.sent-2411-501: Future work can look into ways to ensure descriptions pass a quality threshold.
elements.sent-2411-502: For example, user-driven AD systems can include an automated evaluator module that checks the consistency of AI descriptions with guidelines.
elements.sent-2411-503: Also, a system that allows BLV users to provide feedback and co-create descriptions can help mitigate issues of accuracy, context, and consistency while improving the reliability of AI descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-504: With recent advances in AI descriptions, there is a potential for the roles of novice describers to evolve from creating descriptions to adjusting the timings of receiving descriptions and removing hallucinations and inaccuracies.
elements.sent-2411-505: Although we did not recruit any sighted describers for our study, our results suggest new ways for novice describers to support BLV user needs.
elements.sent-2411-506: While previous efforts have mainly focused on using AI to support novice describers in creating or editing ADs~\cite{image_templates,natalie_feedback,yuksel2020HIML , with newer MLLMs, the role of describers could shift.}
elements.sent-2411-507: In terms of providing visual details, most BLV participants felt the AI descriptions were adequate.
elements.sent-2411-508: However, several participants needed help with knowing when to request descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-509: Instead of describers working to create descriptions, they can work on when to insert descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-510: Some BLV users also had low trust in AI-generated descriptions, which worsened with even small AD inaccuracies.
elements.sent-2411-511: Sighted describers can easily verify the accuracy or detect such hallucinations and inconsistencies and correct them to improve BLV user comprehension, trust, and overall experience.
elements.sent-2411-512: With user-driven AI descriptions, the role of BLV users can also further shift from video consumption to active content creation.
elements.sent-2411-513: Also, compared to sighted novice describers, low vision volunteers have a better idea of when descriptions are needed for a video (e.g., on-screen text, visual reference).
elements.sent-2411-514: While there are already BLV content creators~\cite{digitalContentCreationBLV, avscript , a user-driven AI tool can open up the space to a wider range of BLV users to become AD creators, determining when and how ADs should be inserted for different videos based on their lived experience.}
elements.sent-2411-515: Specifically, AD tools can incorporate the user-driven approach with additional functionalities for BLV users to review, edit, save, and share ADs to enable more than momentary access to descriptions and support BLV people as curators of content that goes beyond their own personal consumption.
elements.sent-2411-516: BLV studies have also highlighted the need for additional output modalities such as audio cues, tactile graphics, and haptics to enhance the AD experience for different viewing scenarios (e.g., how-to, short-form, comedy, drama)~\cite{jiang2024s and support user agency in navigating 360$^\circ$ videos~\cite{jiang360videos .}}
elements.sent-2411-517: Though this requires technology that might not always be available to BLV users, several output modalities can be incorporated for ADs.
elements.sent-2411-518: Brief audio cues, or vibrations, can be incorporated into the video to let the BLV user know when they can activate a description.
elements.sent-2411-519: By adding audio or haptic cues, the control of receiving descriptions still lies with the user, and they have a better idea of when there are scene changes or where they can benefit from activating descriptions, effectively reducing the cognitive load of not knowing when to request descriptions or having too many descriptions in the case of pre-recorded AD.
elements.sent-2411-520: In this regard, MLLMs can help in providing this multi-sensory information.
elements.sent-2411-521: For example, AI can detect scene changes, on-screen text, character changes, visual references, and generate corresponding audio cues and vibrations.
elements.sent-2411-522: The frequency of such feedback can be tailored based on user preferences and customizations.
elements.sent-2411-523: Currently, limited work has been done on the intersection of AD and other modalities, especially how BLV users engage with personal devices (i.e., smartphones) for video consumption~\cite{accessibilityresearch .}
elements.sent-2411-524: Within a 75-minute study, we could only test seven video genres, with one short video per genre.
elements.sent-2411-525: The limited selection restricts the generalizability of our findings, as a lot of variation exists in content within each genre.
elements.sent-2411-526: The pace of the video likely impacted the quantitative results.
elements.sent-2411-527: Testing user-driven AD with other genres, including a broader range of videos with different paces and longer durations, can give further insight into the efficacy of user-driven descriptions across different video types.
elements.sent-2411-528: Second, we created all the descriptions prior to the user study to account for latency and description quality.
elements.sent-2411-529: Third, we did not evaluate whether the generated descriptions fully adhered to the curated guidelines.
elements.sent-2411-530: Our study was based on prior work, which has shown that prompting MLLMs with AD guidelines improved description quality in terms of clarity, accuracy, objectivity, and descriptiveness compared to descriptions generated by either baseline MLLMs or novice describers~\cite{li2025videoa11y .}
elements.sent-2411-531: However, future work should devise mechanisms to measure how well user-driven descriptions comply with the guidelines.
elements.sent-2411-532: Fourth, we investigated BLV people's perception of user-driven ADs in a single session.
elements.sent-2411-533: Lastly, the study was conducted with 20 BLV participants.
elements.sent-2411-534: While this sample provided rich qualitative insights into BLV perceptions of user-driven AI descriptions, the number of participants limits the generalizability of our statistical results.
elements.sent-2411-535: Future work could recruit a larger number of participants to further expand on our findings.
elements.sent-2411-536: We presented an alternative approach to video accessibility by giving more control to the BLV individuals through user-driven descriptions with two types of detail.
elements.sent-2411-537: Based on the study results with 20 BLV participants, user-driven descriptions improved the sense of user control with flexibility of when and how participants receive descriptions but increased cognitive load and fear of missing out (FOMO).
elements.sent-2411-538: BLV users found the descriptions compelling in terms of the detail of visual content and saw a possibility to engage with more types of content through user-driven interaction.
elements.sent-2411-539: There were also concerns about AI's ability to describe certain kinds of genres.
elements.sent-2411-540: The list below shows the complete 42 audio description (AD) guidelines we curated for prompting GPT-4 Vision (GPT-4V) to create descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-541: Each guideline is listed alongside its corresponding source.
elements.sent-2411-542: For our purposes, we adapted the original guidelines by shortening and combining related guidelines for brevity.
elements.sent-2411-543: Avoid over-describing — Do not include non-essential visual details.~\cite{netflix_style_guide 2.}
elements.sent-2411-544: Description should not be opinionated unless content demands it.~\cite{netflix_style_guide 3.}
elements.sent-2411-545: Choose level of detail based on plot relevance when describing scenes.~\cite{netflix_style_guide 4.}
elements.sent-2411-546: Description should be informative and conversational, in present tense and third-person omniscient.~\cite{netflix_style_guide 5.}
elements.sent-2411-547: The vocabulary should reflect the predominant language/accent of the program and should be consistent with the genre and tone of the content while also mindful of the target audience.
elements.sent-2411-548: Vocabulary used should ensure accuracy, clarity, and conciseness.~\cite{netflix_style_guide 6.}
elements.sent-2411-549: Consider historical context and avoid words with negative connotations or bias.~\cite{netflix_style_guide 7.}
elements.sent-2411-550: Pay attention to verbs — Choose vivid verbs over bland ones with adverbs.~\cite{netflix_style_guide 8.}
elements.sent-2411-551: Use pronouns only when clear whom they refer to.~\cite{netflix_style_guide 9.}
elements.sent-2411-552: Use comparisons for shapes and sizes with familiar and globally relevant objects.~\cite{netflix_style_guide 10.}
elements.sent-2411-553: Maintain consistency in word choice, character qualities, and visual elements for all audio descriptions.~\cite{netflix_style_guide 11.}
elements.sent-2411-554: Tone and vocabulary should match the target audience's age range.~\cite{netflix_style_guide 12.}
elements.sent-2411-555: Ensure no errors in word selection, pronunciation, diction, or enunciation.~\cite{dcmp_description_key 13.}
elements.sent-2411-556: Start with general context, then add details.~\cite{dcmp_description_key 14.}
elements.sent-2411-557: Describe shape, size, texture, or color as appropriate to the content.~\cite{dcmp_description_key 15.}
elements.sent-2411-558: Use first-person narrative for engagement if required to engage the audience.~\cite{dcmp_description_key 16.}
elements.sent-2411-559: Use articles appropriately to introduce or refer to subjects.~\cite{dcmp_description_key 17.}
elements.sent-2411-560: Prefer formal speech over colloquialisms, except where appropriate.~\cite{dcmp_description_key 18.}
elements.sent-2411-561: When introducing new terms, objects, or actions, label them first, and then follow with the definitions.~\cite{dcmp_description_key 19.}
elements.sent-2411-562: Describe objectively without personal interpretation or comment.
elements.sent-2411-563: Also, do not censor content.~\cite{dcmp_description_key, ofcom_access_services 20.}
elements.sent-2411-564: Deliver narration steadily and impersonally (but not monotonously), matching the program's tone.~\cite{ofcom_access_services 21.}
elements.sent-2411-565: It can be important to add emotion, excitement, lightness of touch at different points.
elements.sent-2411-566: Adjust style for emotion and mood according to the program's genre.~\cite{ofcom_access_services 22.}
elements.sent-2411-567: If it is children’s content, tailor language and pace for children's TV, considering audience feedback.~\cite{ofcom_access_services \end{minipage \setlength{\fboxsep {8pt \setlength{\fboxrule {0.5mm \fcolorbox{black {blue!2 { \begin{minipage {0.94\linewidth 23.}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-568: Do not alter, filter, or exclude content.
elements.sent-2411-569: You should describe what you see.
elements.sent-2411-570: Try to seek simplicity and succinctness in your description.~\cite{mediaccess 24.}
elements.sent-2411-571: Prioritize what is relevant when describing action as to not affect user experience.~\cite{netflix_style_guide 25.}
elements.sent-2411-572: Include location, time, and weather conditions when relevant to the scene or plot.~\cite{netflix_style_guide 26.}
elements.sent-2411-573: Focus on key content for learning and enjoyment when creating audio descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-574: This is so that the intention of the program is conveyed.~\cite{dcmp_description_key 27.}
elements.sent-2411-575: When describing an instructional video/content, describe the sequence of activities first.~\cite{dcmp_description_key 28.}
elements.sent-2411-576: For a dramatic production, include elements such as style, setting, focus, period, dress, facial features, objects, and aesthetics.~\cite{dcmp_description_key 29.}
elements.sent-2411-577: Describe what is most essential for the viewer to know in order to follow, understand, and appreciate the intended learning outcomes of the video/content.~\cite{dcmp_description_key 30.}
elements.sent-2411-578: Audio description should describe characters, locations, time and circumstances, on-screen action, and on-screen information.~\cite{ofcom_access_services 31.}
elements.sent-2411-579: Describe only what a sighted viewer can see.~\cite{mediaccess 32.}
elements.sent-2411-580: Describe main and key supporting characters' visual aspects relevant to identity and personality.
elements.sent-2411-581: Prioritize factual descriptions of traits like hair, skin, eyes, build, height, age, and visible disabilities.
elements.sent-2411-582: Ensure consistency and avoid singling out characters for specific traits.
elements.sent-2411-584: If unable to confirm or if not established in the plot, do not guess or assume racial, ethnic or gender identity.~\cite{netflix_style_guide 34.}
elements.sent-2411-585: When naming characters for the first time, aim to include a descriptor before the name (e.g., a bearded man, Jack).~\cite{netflix_style_guide 35.}
elements.sent-2411-586: Description should convey facial expressions, body language and reactions.~\cite{netflix_style_guide 36.}
elements.sent-2411-587: When important to the meaning / intent of content, describe race using currently-accepted terminology.~\cite{dcmp_description_key 37.}
elements.sent-2411-588: Avoid identifying characters solely by gender expression unless it offers unique insights not apparent otherwise to visually impaired viewers.~\cite{mediaccess 38.}
elements.sent-2411-589: Describe character clothing if it enhances characterization, plot, setting, or genre enjoyment.~\cite{mediaccess 39.}
elements.sent-2411-590: If text on the screen is central to understanding, establish a pattern of on-screen words being read.
elements.sent-2411-591: This may include making an announcement, such as 'Words appear'.~\cite{mediaccess 40.}
elements.sent-2411-592: In the case of subtitles, the describer should read the translation after stating that a subtitle appears.~\cite{mediaccess 41.}
elements.sent-2411-593: When shot changes are critical to the understanding of the scene, indicate them by describing where the action is or where characters are present in the new shot.~\cite{netflix_style_guide 42.}
elements.sent-2411-594: Provide description before the content rather than after.. ~\cite{netflix_style_guide \end{minipage.}}
elements.sent-2411-595: The following prompt was used to create concise and detailed description using the GPT-4V API.
elements.sent-2411-596: You will watch some keyframes from a video.
elements.sent-2411-597: For each keyframe, you will provide video descriptions, following the guidelines below.
elements.sent-2411-598: The instructions are some of the guidelines to keep in mind when providing descriptions to blind and low vision users.
elements.sent-2411-599: You'll need to use [all] of the guidelines.
elements.sent-2411-600: As these descriptions will be spoken for the second of the video they describe, the descriptions should sound as if they are part of a continuous narrative rather than segmented into distinct frames or images.
elements.sent-2411-601: Do not mention frames in your description.
elements.sent-2411-602: Return each description in a new line.
elements.sent-2411-603: Each audio description should be around [maximum word limit] words.
elements.sent-2411-604: If you cannot provide descriptions, provide a reason as to why.. \end{minipage.}

elements.section-2411-011: Appendix: Interview Questions in Our User Study
elements.sent-2411-608: The following questions were used during the post-session interviews to gather participants’ qualitative feedback on their experience with user-driven interaction.
elements.sent-2411-609: The interview was semi-structured, allowing for follow-up questions based on the responses.
elements.sent-2411-610: How was your experience with user-driven descriptions?.
elements.sent-2411-611: How was the experience of user-driven descriptions as compared to your previous experience with AD?.
elements.sent-2411-612: How did you decide whether to activate a description (i.e., what factors impacted your decision)?.
elements.sent-2411-613: What do you think is the best way to receive descriptions?.
elements.sent-2411-614: I noticed you requested descriptions (frequently/rarely) for (most/some) videos.
elements.sent-2411-615: Was there a reason for that?.
elements.sent-2411-616: If you could have user-driven descriptions for any video, would you have a similar frequency of activations in daily life?.
elements.sent-2411-617: What did you think about the AI descriptions?.
elements.sent-2411-618: How do you think the AI descriptions compare to descriptions created by human describers?.
elements.sent-2411-619: How was your experience of AI voice being used for descriptions?.
elements.sent-2411-620: What are your thoughts about the concise descriptions?.
elements.sent-2411-621: What are your thoughts about the detailed descriptions?.
elements.sent-2411-622: In what situations would you prefer concise descriptions? In what scenarios would you prefer detailed descriptions?.
elements.sent-2411-623: Were there any videos for which you felt the descriptions were good?.
elements.sent-2411-624: Were there any videos in which you felt the descriptions were bad?.
elements.sent-2411-625: How could the descriptions be made better for the videos with bad descriptions?.
elements.sent-2411-626: In the end, do you have any other comments?.
